DEFENDANT APPLE INC., THE ONLY REMAINING DEFENDANT WHO HAS APPEARED IN THE CASE, HAS STIPULATED TO THE RELIEF SOUGHT BY THIS MOTION. LEDCOR MUST FILE ITS FIRST AMENDED COMPLAINT NO LATER THAN AUGUST 15, 2017. IT IS NOT DEEMED FILED. WRITTEN ORDER IS SIGNED AND FILED IN OPEN COURT. JUDGE: HAROLD KAHN; CLERK: J. CASE MANAGEMENT CONFERENCE
Off Calendar Jun-23-2017 Set For Trial On Sep-10-18 At 09:30 A.M. In Dept 206

DEMURRER TO CROSS COMPLAINT
Off Calendar May-16-2017 Sustained
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Louis J. Blum, Esq. of Finch Thornton Baird, Tel. 858.737.3100, for cross defendant and moving party LEDCOR CONSTRUCTION INC.;
Giselle Roohparvar, Esq. of Miller Starr Regalia, Tel. 415.638.4800, for cross complainant and opposing party APPLE, INC.

RULING: The matter was argued. At the conclusion of argument, the court adopted the tentative ruling with modification, as follows: Ledcor Construction Inc.'s demurrer to the third cause of action for negligence in the cross-complaint filed by Apple Inc. is sustained with leave to amend. Apple has failed to adequately allege a claim for negligence because the claim is based on the same conduct supporting Apple's contract claims. "A person may not ordinarily recover in tort for the breach of duties that merely restate contractual obligations." (Aas v. Superior Court (2000) 24 Cal.4th 627, 643.) "[C]onduct amounting to a breach of contract becomes tortious only when it violates a duty independent of the contract arising from principles of tort law. An omission to perform a contract obligation is never a tort, unless that omission is also an omission of a legal duty." (Erlich v. Menezes (1999) 21 Cal.4th 543, 551, citations omitted.) Accordingly, the facts alleged do not show Ledcor owed a legal duty to Apple independent of its contractual obligations. Apple's reliance on North American Chemical Co. v. Superior Court (1997) 59 Cal.App.4th 764 is not persuasive in light of the California Supreme Court's discussion of the case in Aas. (See Aas, 24 Cal.4th at p. 642-43.) Further, Beacon Residential Community Ass'n. v. Skidmore, Owings & Merrill LLP (2014) 59 Cal.4th 568, is distinguishable because there was absence of privity in Beacon and the contract at issue there was an architectural professional services contract. Ledcor's assertion of contract abandonment in its complaint is not relevant to this demurrer. The Court modified and signed the order in open court. Judge: Stephen M. Murphy; Clerk: Gina Gonzales; Court Reporter: Laura Reding, CSR 711
